coredata.dc:title: Biodegradable films based on gum arabic, chitosan, and polyvinyl alcohol incorporating Hibiscus flower-derived carbon dots impact the postharvest quality of Barhi dates
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alamer, Huda Ahmad
Shawir, Samar M.S.
Kamel, Reham M.
Salama, Abdel-Moety
Sakr, Hazem
coredata.dc:description: This research explores incorporating biosynthesized Hibiscus flower carbon dots (HCDs) into gum arabic (G)/chitosan (C)/polyvinyl alcohol (P) (GCP) films for novel bioactive packaging. Hydrothermally synthesized HCDs exhibited spherical morphology (5–7 nm) and characteristic UV absorption (247 nm, 279 nm) with 392 nm photoluminescence (316 nm excitation). HCD incorporation significantly altered GCP film properties. Colorimetric analysis revealed a dose-dependent effect, with increasing HCD concentrations reducing lightness and whiteness index while increasing yellowness and total color difference. Mechanical testing showed optimal tensile strength and elongation at 15 % HCD incorporation, while 25 % HCD increased film thickness. HCDs reduced film solubility and moisture content, with the lowest values at the highest concentration. Antioxidant capacity, assessed by DPPH and ABTS assays, increased with HCD concentration, peaking at 25 %. However, higher HCD loadings increased the water vapor transmission rate. Storage studies using Barhi dates demonstrated superior preservation with 15 % HCD-modified GCP films, effectively retarding firmness loss and minimizing weight loss after 30 days compared to controls and conventional packaging. These findings suggest HCD-reinforced GCP films offer a sustainable strategy for prolonging the period of marketable quality of climacteric fruits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hibiscus flower carbon dots (HCDs)
Hydrothermal synthesis
Bio-nanocomposite films (GCP films)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Hibiscus carbon dots H-CDs
Microscopic analysis of surface and cross-sectional morphology
Water solubility, moisture content, and water vapor permeability assessment
Physical properties (weight loss and firmness evaluation)
Chemical properties (soluble solid content and titratable acidity)
Thermal study of GCP, GCP-HCD1and GCP-HCD2 bio-nanocomposite films
Water solubility, moisture content, and water vapor permeability assessment
Potential for maintaining fruit quality during storage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images and the size distribution of the HCDs.
(a) PL excitation and emission spectra; (b) normalized PL spectra; and (c) UV–VIS absorption, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of HCDs.
(a) SEM; and (b) cross-section SEM of the GCP films, GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 bio-nanocomposite film.
(a) 3D AFM; (b) 2D AFM; and (c) photographic images showing the transparency of the GCP films, GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 bio-nanocomposite film.
(a) XRD; (b) FTIR; (c) DSC; (d) WCA, and (e) UV–Vis transmittance of GCP, GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 bio-nanocomposite film.
Photographic record of Barhi dates under various treatments over a 30-day period.
(a) Weight loss; (b) firmness; (c) SSC; and (d) TA of Barhi dates.
Conceptual diagram outlining the GCP/HCDs bio-nanocomposite film compositional.
Optical parameters of the prepared composite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are presented as mean ± standard deviation, different letters in the same column indicate significant differences among films (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanical and antioxidant properties of the prepared bio-nanocomposite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are presented as mean ± standard deviation, different letters in the same column indicate significant differences among films (P < 0.05).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are presented as mean ± standard deviation, different letters in the same column indicate significant differences among films (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Biodegradable films based on gum arabic, chitosan, and polyvinyl alcohol incorporating Hibiscus flower-derived carbon dots impact the postharvest quality of Barhi dates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Horticulture, Faculty of Agriculture, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafr El-Shaikh 33516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agricultural Research Center, Food Technology Research Institute, Giza 12611, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Agricultural Research Center, Food Technology Research Institute, Giza 12611, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Food Technology Research Institute, Agricultural Research Center, Giza, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research explores incorporating biosynthesized Hibiscus flower carbon dots (HCDs) into gum arabic (G)/chitosan (C)/polyvinyl alcohol (P) (GCP) films for novel bioactive packaging. Hydrothermally synthesized HCDs exhibited spherical morphology (5–7 nm) and characteristic UV absorption (247 nm, 279 nm) with 392 nm photoluminescence (316 nm excitation). HCD incorporation significantly altered GCP film properties. Colorimetric analysis revealed a dose-dependent effect, with increasing HCD concentrations reducing lightness and whiteness index while increasing yellowness and total color difference. Mechanical testing showed optimal tensile strength and elongation at 15 % HCD incorporation, while 25 % HCD increased film thickness. HCDs reduced film solubility and moisture content, with the lowest values at the highest concentration. Antioxidant capacity, assessed by DPPH and ABTS assays, increased with HCD concentration, peaking at 25 %. However, higher HCD loadings increased the water vapor transmission rate. Storage studies using Barhi dates demonstrated superior preservation with 15 % HCD-modified GCP films, effectively retarding firmness loss and minimizing weight loss after 30 days compared to controls and conventional packaging. These findings suggest HCD-reinforced GCP films offer a sustainable strategy for prolonging the period of marketable quality of climacteric fruits.

ce:section.ce:para: The prevalent inclination toward the adoption of petroleum-based plastic packaging has resulted in significant challenges, as these packaging materials exhibit a prolonged decomposition timeline spanning hundreds of years, thereby contributing to severe environmental pollution and health-related issues. Moreover, contemporary food packaging technology effectively safeguards food quality; however, it remains insufficient in preventing losses attributable to photooxidation and microbial contamination during manufacturing, preservation, and distribution [1]. Consequently, given the escalating consumer apprehension regarding the sustainability of food supplies, product safety standards, quality parameters, and ecological consequences, the development of financially efficient and ecologically responsible benign high-performance food packaging materials, particularly bio-decomposable and functional packaging materials endowed with bacteriostatic and radical scavenging activity, is imperative [2].

Biogenic raw materials, including biomacromolecules, are abundantly available on a planetary scale. Their sustainability, renewability, bio-decomposability, biocompatibility, inherently safe characteristics, and environmental benignity position them as striking alternatives to petroleum-derived polymer packaging [3]. When combined with appropriate bio-based additives and fillers at the nanoscale, these biopolymers enhance material integrity and protective qualities.

Chitosan (C), a positively charged carbohydrate polymer, is a frequently employed material for the fabrication of edible films characterized by their biocompatibility, biodegradability, antioxidant properties, and commendable film-forming capacity [4]. Gum Arabic (G), a negatively charged heteropolysaccharide, is a non-toxic substance characterized by its approximately 2 % protein content, which is covalently bound to the polysaccharide structure. Polyvinyl alcohol (P), a biocompatible, water-soluble polymer, plays a significant role in food packaging and various other sectors owing to its remarkable mechanical qualities, solvent resistance, and biocompatibility, coupled with a diminished rate of biological decomposition., despite its significant degree of moisture absorption [5].

Promising sustainable food packaging, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a topic of significant interest in recent years as functional nanofillers dependent on their hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and facile synthesis. Their recently revealed UV-attenuating, radical-scavenging, and bactericidal activities further emphasize their importance as active packaging materials [6]. Carbon dots (CDs) are currently finding widespread application in diverse optoelectronic and biomedical applications, including bioimaging, optical instrumentation, photocatalysis, drug delivery systems, and biochemical sensing, attributable to their advantageous characteristics of cost-effectiveness, synthetic accessibility, high aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and consistent photoluminescence [7].

The date palm (Phoenix dactylifera L.) boasts one of the longest histories of fruit cultivation globally, especially in the Arabian Peninsula, the Middle East, North Africa, and high-temperature xeric zones. Its designation as a “tree of life” reflects its adaptability, drought tolerance, sustained productivity, and numerous applications. Due to its climacteric nature, date fruit maturation is associated with ethylene-induced physiological changes, resulting in four distinct stages (Arabic terms): Kimri (hard, green, immature), Khalal (hard, yellow, edible Barhi), Rutab (soft, brown, semi-ripe), and Tamar (soft, dark brown, fully ripe). The Khalal stage of the popular Barhi date variety—distinguished by its sweet taste and yellow hue—is transient post-harvest, typically transitioning to Rutab within a week if not handled appropriately. The market demand for Khalal Barhi dates, driven by their unique organoleptic properties and health benefits conferred by phenolics and other bioactive compounds, underscores the economic significance of this fresh, yellow fruit [8].

In this study, multifunctional bio-nanocomposite films were fabricated using a blend of biocompatible and biodegradable polysaccharides: Gum Arabic, chitosan, and PVA (GCP) (see Scheme 1 ). These biopolymers were selected for their inherent bioactivity and film-forming capabilities, with chitosan contributing to antimicrobial activity, gum Arabic providing excellent film-forming, emulsifying properties, and antioxidant potential, and PVA's contribution to mechanical strength and barrier properties. HCDs were then incorporated as active reinforcing agents and their ability to scavenge free radicals.

It was hypothesized that the synergistic interactions of Gum Arabic, chitosan, and PVA within the GCP matrix would result in robust bio-nanocomposite films exhibiting enhanced structural integrity, barrier properties, and antioxidant activity. Furthermore, it was hypothesized that incorporating HCDs would further increase antioxidant activity and impart UV-blocking properties on Barhi dates stored with the films, thereby improving their preservation by reducing weight loss and maintaining fruit quality.

Date palm (P. dactylifera L.) and Hibiscus Sabdariffa flowers (roselle plant) were purchased from the Egyptian local market. All chemicals were of analytical reagent (AR) grade and purified water was used in all steps of this research to prepare the solutions. Gum Arabic (Acacia gum), Chitosan (Mw3600, >90 % deacetylation, 100–200 cP) was procured from Sigma Chemical Co.), Acetic acid glacial (TEDIA company- high purity), polyvinyl alcohol extra pure (PVA, MW: 31,000–50,000, 94%purity and 99.0–99.9 % hydrolyzed), Glycerol (M = 92.1 g/mol; purity (GC) ≥ 99.0 %). Deionized water, (DPPH)2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazine, and (ABTS) (2,2-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Hibiscus carbon dots H-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesized through hydrothermal carbonization of Hibiscus Sabdariffa flowers that were initially washed, dried, and subsequently pulverized using a Moulinex mill for a 2-min duration. A 10-g sample was homogeneously dispersed within 100 ml of deionized water and subjected to overnight agitation. Subsequently, the solution was enclosed within a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and subjected to a reaction of 180 °C for 12 h, permitted to attain ambient temperature. The resultant product was filtered by Whatman paper, centrifuged, and further refined through syringe filtration (0.22 μm) to maroon yield uniform, diminutive H-CDs.

The surface topography of the carbon dots was characterized through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) utilizing a Tecnai F30 microscope operated at a working voltage of 300 kV. The Nano Measurer 1.2.5 software was employed to determine the mean particle size and size distribution. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the carbon dots were recorded using an Analytik Jena Specord S600 spectrophotometer, while their photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained with a Techcomp FL970 photoluminescent spectrophotometer.

Three solutions were prepared. A 2 % chitosan solution (A) was formed by dispersing 2 g of chitosan within 100 ml of distilled water containing 0.5 % glacial acetic acid and stirring continuously overnight. Subsequently, a gum Arabic solution (B) was created by dissolving 1 g of gum arabic powder in 50 ml of distilled water under constant agitation at 80 °C for 20 min and then adding 50 ml of cooled distilled water under constant agitation at 50 °C for 100 min. Lastly, a separate solution (C) was prepared by dissolving 4 g grams of polyvinyl alcohol powder in 100 ml of distilled water through stirring for 4 h at 95 °C.

A chitosan-gum Arabic film was fabricated by combining solutions A, B, and C in a volumetric ratio of 1:2.5:0.5 under magnetic agitation for 30 min. Subsequently, a 1 % (w/v) glycerol solution was incorporated into the mixture and stirred for an additional 90 min at 50 °C under 850 rpm stirring. A 120 ml aliquot of the resulting solution was then cast into a Pyrex dish and subjected to desiccation by exposure to air conditioning at a temperature of 60 °C and medium airflow for a period of 24 h. The dried film was subsequently characterized.

Carbon dot solutions at varying concentrations of 15 and 25 % (v/v) were incorporated into the previously prepared chitosan-gum arabic solution, resulting in two distinct film-forming solutions (see Scheme 1). 120 ml quantity of each solution was subsequently cast into a Pyrex dish and then desiccated prior to characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic analysis of surface and cross-sectional morphology
ce:section.ce:para: Film microstructure was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Sirion 200, FEI, Netherlands) using gold sputter-coated, desiccated, and sectioned (small squares) samples, with micrographs acquired at 3000× and 1600× (5 kV).

Film samples, after standardized sectioning to appropriate dimensions, were analyzed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd. (USA) FTIR spectrometer (500–4000 cm−1 spectral range, 8 cm−1 resolution).

A PANalytical X'Pert diffractometer (Netherlands), configured with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 Å) and a nickel monochromator, was employed to determine the XRD patterns of the film samples. The instrument was operated at 40 kV and 30 mA, and data were recorded over a 2θ range of 5° to 40° at a scan rate of 0.4°/min at room temperature.

The crystallinity index (CI%) was derived using the following mathematical expression:
(1)
CI = (
I 200
−
I
a m
/
I 200
) × 100
where I200 was the integrated intensity at the plane (200), which accounted for the highest peak intensity of the crystalline fractions. In contrast, Iam was the minimum intensity that depicted the amorphous phase.

The crystallite size of films was derived using the Scherrer equation:
(2)
D = K
λ /
β
1 / 2
c o s θ

The method employed a prescribed formula that included various parameters, such as the Scherrer constant (0.94), X-ray wavelength (λ = 0.15406 Å), Bragg's angle (θ), and the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the XRD peak (β 1/2). The diffractograms were modified and the contribution of the amorphous peak (Iam) was removed from the peak height of the plane (200).

Using a SHIMADZU DSC-60 Plus instrument, DSC analysis was performed on film fragments (3–5 mg) contained in flattened aluminum pans, under a 50 cm3/min nitrogen purge (99.999 %), with a heating ramp from 0 °C to 200 °C at 10 °C/min.

AFM(model FLEXAFM3) was used, the scan area was 10 × 10μm2, and the number of data points: was 256 × 256 at a scan rate:1HZ. The AFM was operated in contact mode using a nanoconductive silicon probe using Nanosurf C3000(version 3.5.0.31) software.

To assess the film's capacity to obstruct the transmission of ultraviolet and visible light (UV–vis), its transmittance was quantified within the spectral range of 200 to 800 nm utilizing an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Jena specord S600, Germany). The films, incorporating varying proportions of HCDs, were sectioned into strips measuring 1 × 4 cm and positioned within a quartz cuvette to obtain the respective transmission curves in the UV–vis region [9].

The water contact angle (WCA) values of the GCP/GCP-HCD films were evaluated by means of a contact angle goniometer (HARKESPCAX1, HARKE, China) via the sessile drop technique. Film samples were affixed to glass slides using double-sided tape and deionized water droplets were carefully deposited on the film surface. The droplet contours were subsequently captured and analyzed to determine the WCA values. This process was replicated four times for each film sample to ensure accuracy and consistency.

Colorimetric analysis was conducted on each sample at five distinct regions using a Minolta color reader, yielding L*, a*, and b* values. These values were then used to calculate the total color difference (ΔE) and whiteness index (WI) according to Eqn 3 and 4.
(3)
ΔE =
L ∗
−
L 0
2
+
a ∗
−
a 0
2
+
b ∗
−
b 0
2
where L* signifies initial values, while L0 signifies the final values.
(4)
WI =
100 −
100 − L
2
+
a 2
+
b 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water solubility, moisture content, and water vapor permeability assessment
ce:section.ce:para:
The extent of film water solubility was quantified using the method reported by Valizadeh, Naseri [10]. The mass loss of oven-dried film specimens (2 × 2 cm2) after immersion in 50 mL of distilled water with agitation at 25 °C for 24 h and subsequent drying at 105 °C was used to calculate the solubility percentage according to the following formula:
(5)
Water Solubility
%
= (
I M
−
F M
/
I M
) × 100
‘IM’ and ‘FM’ are the film sample's initial and final after being dried.

The moisture content of the films was quantified by measuring the mass loss upon drying at 105 °C for 24 h in an electric oven. The initial and final masses of the films were recorded, and the moisture content was calculated according to the following equation:
(6)
Moisture Content
%
= (
I M
−
F M
/
I M
) × 100
‘IM’ and ‘FM’ are the film sample's initial and final after being dried.

The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) was quantified using the standardized ASTM E398 method. Before measurement, samples (60 mm diameter) were conditioned at 25 % RH and 25 °C for 24 h to ensure the establishment of steady-state conditions.

Mechanical testing, to determine tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB), was performed using an Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA) following ASTM D 882–88. The testing apparatus was configured with a 50 mm initial grip separation and a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min.

Antioxidant activity of composite film extracts prepared by extracting 80 mg of film in 1.5 mL of 50:50 ethanol/water for 24 h was quantified using DPPH (150 μL of extract, 50 μL of 0.15 mM methanolic DPPH, and absorbance at 517 nm, a 30-min incubation under dark conditions) and ABTS assays (150 μL of extract, 50 μL pre-generated radical cation solution, Absorbance at 734 nm, 30 min dark incubation). The ABTS radical cation solution was generated by mixing equal volumes of 140 mM ABTS and 4.9 mM potassium persulfate and storing overnight in the dark antioxidant activity could be quantified as implied by Eqn 7:
(7)
Scavenging ability
%
= (
C A
−
S A
/
C A
) × 100
In this context, CA and SA indicates the control and sample absorbance values, respectively.

Date palm fruits (P. dactylifera L.) were obtained from a local Egyptian market and selected for homogeneity in color and size, lack of defects, and fungal damage, and were processed without delay upon arrival at the laboratory. The fruits were washed with chlorinated water (50 mg/L) for 5 min, subsequently, the treated fruits were gently wiped with a clean cotton cloth and air-dried at ambient temperature for 30 min. Date fruits (n = 10 per film type) were wrapped in polyethylene (PolyEth), GCP, GCP-HCD1, or GCP-HCD2 films and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical properties (weight loss and firmness evaluation)
ce:section.ce:para:
Quantification of weight loss (WL) after 15 and 30 days of storage was performed using the following equation:
(8)
WL
%
= (
W 0
−
W 1
/
W 0
) × 100
where W1 represents the weight of Barhi dates at time points 15 and 30 days and W0 represents the weight at the initial time point (0 days).
The firmness of the samples was evaluated using a plus texture analyzer (SMS, UK) with a 1.0 mm/s compression velocity during the entire test protocol and a 7 mm compression distance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical properties (soluble solid content and titratable acidity)
ce:section.ce:para: Soluble solid content (SSC) was assessed via refractometry by a digital hand refractometer (model Palette, PR-32, ATAGO, Saitama, Japan) at ambient temperature.

The titratable acidity (expressed as citric acid %) was determined by extracting the known weight of the sample into distilled water and titrating with 0.1 N sodium hydroxide, using phenolphthalein as an indicator to an endpoint of pH 8.1 (AOAC, 2000).
Statistical analysis was conducted by one-way ANOVA (SPSS v. 21; Triplicate data, Duncan's test, p ≤ 0.05) under a completely randomized design. Data are presented as means ± SD.

To substantiate the formation of Hibiscus carbon dots, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images and the size distribution of the synthesized HCDs were visualized in Fig. 1 . The TEM unequivocally reveals the almost spherical morphology and well-dispersed nature of the synthesized CDs. Moreover, the size distribution of the CDs predominantly indicates that a substantial proportion of the particles exhibited a diameter of <10 nm, ranging from 4.05 to 8.95 nm. A significant majority, approximately 80 % of the particles, were observed to have diameters within the range of 4.75 to 6.85 nm, as determined by particle size analysis [11].

To shed light on the PL mechanisms of HCDs, a range of detailed characterization techniques was employed. The fluorescent behavior of HCDs was extensively studied revealing that distinct blue luminescence was exhibited by a HCDs water-based solution upon exposure to ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm) while appearing transparent under daylight conditions, as depicted in Fig. S1 [inset: (b) and (a)], respectively.

Fig. 2a showcases the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of carbon dots, highlighting their excitation dependence on both emission and intensity. Emission spectra of H-CDs obtained using excitation wavelengths from 280 to 460 nm increments in 20 nm increments, showed an initial increase in emission magnitude with rising excitation wavelength, peaking at 392 nm when excited at 320 nm. However, this excitation-dependent photoluminescence, manifested as a redshift in emission intensity with increasing excitation wavelength, is attributed to diverse surface functional sites and particle size variations [12].

The normalized intensity of the emission peaks, when represented graphically in relation to wavelength, demonstrates a distinct trend of redshift with increasing excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is well-established as the excitation-dependent emission behavior characteristic of carbon dots, as revealed in Fig. 2b [13].

The spectral curve of carbon dots absorption, depicted in Fig. 2c, exhibits two maxima at 247 nm and 279 nm, attributed to π-π and n-π transition of CC bond and CO bond, respectively, thus conferring ultraviolet radiation absorption capacity to the HCDs [14]. Furthermore, Fig. 2c demonstrates that the HCDs were observed to have a maximal inducement wavelength of 316 nm and a maximal emission wavelength of 392 nm, thereby offering a potential strategy for safeguarding food from ultraviolet-induced spoilage when integrated into packaging films.
In summary, the Figure effectively depicts the distinctive photoluminescent behavior of carbon dots, often attributed to their unique electronic structure and surface properties.

Microstructural analysis of the surface and cross-section of GCP films (Fig. 3a–b) provided key insights into their physical, barrier, and mechanical attributes. The absence of discontinuities such as pores and cracks in GCP film indicated satisfactory compatibility between gum Arabic, chitosan, and PVA. However, a clear progression in surface roughness was observed upon HCD incorporation. The GCP film displayed a smooth, compact, and uniform microstructure, whereas all GCP-HCD films exhibited textured surfaces and cross-sections. The GCP-HCD2 film, exhibiting the highest surface particle density and most pronounced surface roughness with distinct furrows in both surface and cross-section, also demonstrated a direct correlation between carbon dot concentration and film thickness (91.4–205.8 μm; Fig. 3b, Table 2). This augmented thickness suggests that HCDs induced a denser, more compact GCP matrix structure, indicative of physical interactions, notably hydrogen bonding, between the components [15–17].

AFM (3D and 2D) provided direct evidence that HCD incorporation increased the surface roughness of GCP films. Statistically significant (P < 0.05) increases in both Arithmetic mean roughness (Ra) and root mean square roughness (Rq) values were observed with increasing HCD concentration (Fig. 4a–b). The GCP-HCD1 film (15 % HCDs) presented Ra and Rq values of 21.06 nm and 31.09 nm, respectively, compared to the control GCP film (12.7 nm and 18.8 nm). These findings were corroborated by surface and cross-sectional morphological analysis (Fig. 3a–b), which also revealed a more pronouncedly rough surface and the presence of protrusions in the GCP-HCD2 films, consistent with their higher HCD content and elevated Ra and Rq values (50.8 nm and 81.8 nm, respectively).

As depicted in Fig. 4c, GCP packaging membranes presented a faint yellowish coloration and a wrinkled surface texture. The incorporation of HCDs induced a concentration-dependent intensification of the yellowish-brown coloration [18]. It is pertinent to mention that these colorimetric findings correspond with the fluorescent properties of the HCDs, as evidenced by Fig. S1. Concurrently, the films displayed improved transparency, and the surface wrinkles were mitigated, more cohesive structure [19]. Moreover, the HCD-containing films demonstrated a noticeable increase in thickness relative to the GCP films, as clearly visualized in the cross-sectional images in Fig. 3b. Similarly, Bao, Hu [9] observed a similar trend, reporting an increase in film thickness with increasing CD concentration in gelatin films, attributed to the addition of solid matter.

The test was conducted to investigate the changes in GCP, GCP-HCD1, as well as GCP-HCD2 films and the data are presented in Fig. 5a. The significant diffraction peak at 2θ = 19.82° in the GCP sample indicates a degree of order within the matrix, arising from semi-crystalline regions formed by interactions among the components. This peak position is associated with intermolecular spacing influenced by hydrogen bonding and molecular packing. Xu, Gao [4] reported that GA and CS exhibited interactions at the crystalline level, which were further intensified with increasing GA proportions, leading to the formation of globular microstructures alongside molecular entanglements and hindering the development of individual polymer crystalline structures. The slight shift in the diffraction peak from 2θ = 19.82° and 19.78° for GCP and GCP-HCD1 to 2θ = 20.06° for GCP-HCD2 suggests subtle structural modifications and potential changes in crystallinity, intermolecular spacing, or molecular order within the polymer matrix due to increasing HCD content [20].

When contrasted with the GCP film, the elevation in peak intensity from 286 to 290 (a.u.) exhibited by GCP-HCD1 indicates that HCD was effectively distributed within the pre-existing GCP film structure, as evidenced by the enhanced band intensity in Fig. 5a [9]. This slight increase in peak intensity suggests that at 15 % HCD concentration, the carbon dots integrate into the film matrix without significantly disrupting its overall order. This interpretation aligns with the crystallinity index (CI) data, where GCP and GCP-HCD1 both exhibit a CI of 75 %, demonstrating that the addition of 15 % HCD does not alter the film's crystallinity. Moreover, the increase in crystallite size from 1.41 nm in GCP to 1.73 nm in GCP-HCD1 suggests that the carbon dots might be acting as nucleating agents, promoting the growth of crystalline domains without compromising the overall degree of crystallinity in Table S1. Conversely, the notable diminution in peak intensity to 270 (a.u.) observed in GCP-HCD2 films suggests that the augmented addition of HCDs may impede the pre-existing GCP structure and compromise intramolecular associations [9]. This disruption of the film structure is further substantiated by the decrease in CI to 70.7 % in GCP-HCD2, indicating a reduction in crystallinity. While the crystallite size remains relatively large at 1.72 nm, the decrease in CI suggests that the higher concentration of HCDs interferes with the formation of ordered crystalline regions within the film.

FTIR spectroscopy offers the potential to reveal structural differences between GCP and HCDs within the GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 films. The pertinent data of spectral filed on the main chemical moieties was from 4000 and 400 cm−1, with the characteristic absorption bands evident in the FTIR (Fig. 5b) at about 3280, 2923, 2876, 1655, 1572, 1418, 1382, 1323, and 1026 cm−1.

A broad peak at approximately 3290 cm−1 in GCP films, attributed to hydroxyl and amino groups, redshifted to 3280 cm−1 and 3278 cm−1 for GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2, respectively. Additionally, the sharper peak at 3278 cm−1 in GCP-HCD2, coupled with increased transmittance, indicates enhanced hydrogen bonding between carbon dots and the matrix, suggesting effective carbon dot integration and a stabilized hydrogen-bond network [21]. The 2923–2876 cm−1 band was attributed to the Saturated hydrocarbon group [22], merging into a single peak for GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2, suggesting an interaction between carbon dots and the host matrix that influences CH bond vibrations.

The absorption band position at 1323 cm−1 is tentatively assigned to CH bending in aliphatic groups or CN stretching in amino-containing components such as chitosan. The emergence of new peaks at 1259 cm−1 and 1262 cm−1 in GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 films indicates significant interactions between carbon dots and CO, C-O-C, and CN groups within the matrix, likely involving hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions.

The FTIR spectrum reveals characteristic peaks at approximately 1655, 1572, 1418, 1382, 1323, and 1026 cm−1, which are attributed to functional groups within the film components, including CO stretching, NH bending, CH bending, CN stretching, and CO stretching vibrations, confirming the presence and interaction of Gum Arabic, chitosan, and PVA within the film matrix [22,23]. Conversely, for GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2, the peaks at approximately 1655, 1572, 1418, and 1382 cm−1 exhibited increased intensity and slight displacements of the peak positions, particularly the more intense peaks at 1655 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1 in GCP-HCD2, suggesting stronger bonds between the carbon dots and the film matrix [24].

These interactions suggest that carbon dots are well-integrated into the matrix, altering their bonding environment and enhancing their structural integrity. FTIR spectra revealed slight displacements of the peak positions upon the addition of HCD to GCP films, indicating the occurrence of intermolecular interactions between GCP and HCD [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal study of GCP, GCP-HCD1and GCP-HCD2 bio-nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5c and Table S1 depict the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) thermograms of GCP and GCP-HCD1, GCP-HCD2 films, represented as a plot of heat flow versus temperature °C. This graphical representation provides valuable insights into the glass transition temperature of the films, a metric that reflects the energy required for water evaporation, thereby indicating the films' moisture content. Chiefly, it was observed that the glass transition temperature (Tg) of pristine GCP film peaked at about 104 °C as a broad endothermic peak was slightly lowered in the case of GCP-HCD1, GCP-HCD2 owing to the dispersion of HCDs within the matrix and dropped to 95 °C and 97 °C. The thermal profile further revealed the degradation profile of both films [26]. Moreover, as documented in Table S1, the variation in enthalpy (ΔH) of the films demonstrated a diminution with the gradual introduction of HCDs, achieving a magnitude of 23.9 J/g in GCP-HCD1, and thereafter exhibiting an elevation to 53.05 J/g in the GCP-HCD2 film. The results obtained implied a deficiency in thermal stability and biological compatibility between the GCP matrix and the HCDs. Due to the more compact network architecture of the GCP-HCD2 film and the HCDs aggregation, higher thermal energy was required to induce degradation, consequently elevating the temperature at which water loss occurred. This effect was validated by the results obtained from the SEM cross-section (Fig. 3b), the XRD profile (Fig. 5a), and the WVP values (Table 3) [27].

Fig. 5e and Table 1 present the transmittance spectra of the fabricated GCP films in the UV–Vis region as a function of HCD loading. The integration of HCD within the GCP matrix engenders a marked diminution in UV transmittance, enhancing the UV-shielding attributes of the films. The GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 composite films, in particular, exhibit superior UV-B blocking capabilities, which can be attributed to the photoluminescence emission of the HCD [28]. The pristine GCP film exhibited negligible visible light absorption, appearing highly transparent with a visible light transmittance of 98.28 ± 0.2 % at 660 nm (T660). While the addition of HCD slightly decreased the optical transmittance of the GCP film owing to scattering and obstruction by HCD particles, the HCD-incorporated films exhibited high transmittance, with a T660 of 95.83 ± 0.2 % even in the GCP-HCD2 film, likely a consequence of the significant reduction in HCD size (<10 nm) in comparison with the wavelength of light (660 nm), which did not impede light transmission [29]. The pristine GCP film exhibited inherent UV-blocking properties due to the polymer's intrinsic UV absorption. The addition of HCD significantly enhanced UV-blocking capabilities in the 200–400 nm wavelength range, resulting in exceptional UV-shielding properties for the HCD-incorporated films. Specifically, GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 films blocked approximately 99.03 ± 0.2 % and 99.13 ± 0.1 % of UV radiation, respectively. These findings align with previous research on Chitosan/Gelatin-CD films and suggest that GCP-HCD films can effectively protect packaged products from UV-induced degradation [2,14,18].

Water contact angle evaluation is widely used as a means of assessing surface wettability. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, GCP film possessed a WCA of 87.9°, while the addition of HCDs in GCP-HCD1 slightly increased the WCA to 90°. A further increase in HCD concentration in GCP-HCD2 resulted in a higher WCA value of 97.2°. This can be attributed to the highly reactive moiety of HCDs, which can readily form new H-bonds with the GCP matrix, facilitating physical cross-linking and inhibiting hydrophilic interaction with water, thereby reducing hydrophilicity [30,31]. This mechanism contributes to the demonstrably significant elevation in the water contact angle (WCA) of the composite films upon HCD incorporation (p < 0.05). This increase can be attributed to the formation of a complex network structure between the abundant hydrophilic groups present in HCDs and the GCP matrix, resulting in a diminution of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups at the film surface. Furthermore, the WCA values obtained were likely influenced by the crystallinity of HCDs and the surface roughness of the film [25,32]. Observations from FTIR, XRD, and AFM analyses revealed that the crystallinity and rough surface of the HCDs-containing films were enhanced, factors that contributed to the increased hydrophobicity of film surface and, consequently, the augmentation of the films' WCA.

The aesthetic and light transmission properties of films, crucial for consumer perception and protecting food from photochemical deterioration, are often modified by incorporating active agents to impart specific functional attributes. Given the inherent brown coloration of HCD, the integration of HCD into composite films can induce a visually perceptible color alteration. The optical properties and visual attributes of the GCP films are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 5e. The exterior hue of the GCP films was substantially influenced by the amount of added HCD. As the HCD concentration rose, a concomitant decrease in lightness (L*) and whiteness index (WI) was observed [33], while redness (a*) and yellowness (b*) values increased, resulting in darker, more brownish hue. Consequently, the overall color difference (ΔE) of the films increased, primarily attributable to the inherent brown coloration of the HCDs, while the incorporation of HCDs imparted a tan hue to the film, a visual characteristic evident in the depicted photo of the film in Fig. 5e. These findings are consistent with the results reported by Ezati and Rhim [34] and Khan, Priyadarshi [28].

A comparison of the thickness values for the GCP film and GCP/H-CDs composite films, as evidenced by Table 2 and Fig. 3b, revealed that the thickness increased with increasing HCDs concentration, with the maximum value of 205.8 μm achieved at a 25 % HCD concentration compared to GCP films. The observation was validated by Bao, Hu [9], who reported a positive correlation between the thickness of the composite film and the content of CDs.

Evaluation of the films' mechanical behavior was performed through measurement of tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB), which are indicative of their flexibility and strength. The results of the films exhibited variations in response to the varying concentrations of H-CDs. The tensile strength remained unchanged with increasing H-CD concentration, while the elongation at break exhibited a biphasic response, initially increasing and then decreasing. The GCP film and GCP-HCD1 and GCP-HCD2 films exhibited tensile strength (TS) values of 11.90 ± 0.75 MPa, 12.32 ± 2.43 MPa, and 11.81 ± 1.26 MPa, respectively [35]. Sakloetsakun, Preechagoon [36] reported that all Chitosan-Gum Arabic films exhibited poor elongation (12 %–20 %), while GCP and GCP-HCD1 films demonstrated improved elongation at break (47.69 % -75.88 %); however, the GCP-HCD2 film exhibited the lowest EB among the films, with a value of 32.25 ± 2.00 %, approximating the results of Sakloetsakun, Preechagoon [36]. The inclusion of HCDs did not elicit a statistically insignificant (P > 0.05) augmentation in the tensile strength of the GCP matrix, showing a modest increment from 11.92 MPa (GCP) to 12.32 MPa (GCP-HCD1), followed by a decrement to 11.81 MPa (GCP-HCD2). Considering surface morphological evaluations of the GCP-based active films, this observation may be ascribed to deficient interfacial compatibility and localized coalescence of HCDs, predominantly observed in GCP-HCD2, which yielded a value of 11.81 MPa. These results are further validated by XRD and AFM analyses [31]. GCP films with 15 % HCDs showed unchanged tensile strength despite a slight thickness increase. XRD revealed nucleating activity (increased crystallite size) and polymer ordering, with consistent crystallinity. The observed increase in elongation might suggest that HCDs functioned as a plasticizer, increasing flexibility without compromising maximum force resistance. Conversely, a 25 % HCD loading induced a significant increase in film thickness, signifying a substantial modification in the film's bulk structure, potentially leading to increase rigidity. XRD analysis revealed a decrease in both the crystallinity index and peak intensity, suggesting a disruption of the GCP matrix's crystalline order. This disruption, likely attributable to HCD aggregation, created structural weaknesses within the film. The slight decrease in crystallite size, coupled with the reduced crystallinity index, indicated that the HCDs were interfering with the formation of well-ordered crystalline regions [37]. The augmentation of HCDs loading to 25 % resulted in a notable decline in elasticity, whilst tensile strength experienced a numerical reduction that did not attain statistical significance. This effect was ascribable to the substantial coalescence of nanosized particles within the GCP film matrix. These observations were in concordance with data derived from thickness assessments, SEM, XRD, and AFM analyses [30].

Antioxidant films represent a significant category of active packaging and a promising approach for extending the shelf life of food products [31]. Table 2 depicted HCDs influence on GCP film antioxidant properties, assessed by quantifying DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging. GCP films exhibited modest scavenging (25.49 % DPPH, 26.21 % ABTS). HCD incorporation significantly enhanced composite film radical scavenging activity concentration-dependently. CP-HCD1 scavenged 75.19 % of DPPH and 80.67 % of ABTS, while GCP-HCD2 showed even greater scavenging, reaching 86.39 % for DPPH and 89.19 % for ABTS [31]. A direct relationship was found between the antioxidant activity of the bio-nanocomposite films and HCD amount, confirming that the HCDs were primarily responsible for the enhanced antioxidant attributes of the films [38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water solubility, moisture content, and water vapor permeability assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding film solubility, a reduction was observed, predominantly with the highest HCD concentration, declining from 33.75 % to 21.34 % as the HCD concentration increased (Table 3 ), which is considered advantageous for food packaging applications [39,40]. This reduction in solubility might be attributed to the increased surface roughness and hydrophobicity imparted by HCDs, as corroborated by morphological analysis.

A parallel pattern was discerned in the predicament of moisture content of GCP films, with decreasing moisture content as HCDs concentration increased. This implies the construction of crosslinks and networks between GCP and HCD molecules, where the interaction between GCP and HCD hydroxyl groups reduces the availability of hydroxyl groups for water interaction [41].

As shown in Table 3, the water vapor permeability (WVP) of the unadulterated GCP film was 1351.25 g/m2. day. The blending of HCDs slightly diminished the WVP of the GCP films. However, a statistically significant increase (p < 0.05) was observed for GCP-HCD2, which contained the maximum HCD incorporation, potentially as a result of the formation of H-bonds with the matrix [42]. Also, another explanation could be attributed to the quantum size of the HCDs, which may not obstruct the free space of the polymer chains or interfere with the tortuous path of water molecules [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential for maintaining fruit quality during storage

ce:section.ce:para: The results obtained, presented in Fig. 7a, demonstrate an increase in weight loss with an extended storage period for all samples. Visual changes in the Barhi dates during storage, as shown in Fig. 6 , complement these weight loss and firmness data. Evaluation of the data showed that the highest degree of weight attrition in Barhi date during storage, attaining a maximum at 30 days, was found in the control group(10.52 %), followed by those preserved with GCP-HCD2 film(8.81 %); conversely, the lowest degree of weight attrition was observed with PolyEth and GCP-HDC1 film, followed by GCP film, which corresponded to 5.46, 6.11 % and 7.22 %, respectively. These outcomes aligned with the observations of Abu-Shama, Abou-Zaid [43], who reported an elevation in Barhi date weight attrition from 0 % at baseline to 6.51 %, and 14.83 % with incremental increases throughout the storage period (2, and 4 weeks, respectively) at 6 °C. The primary mechanism for weight decrement was the loss of water vapor through transpiration and the catabolism of carbon via respiration. The application of GCP and GCP-HCD1 films to the fruit provided selectively permeable interfaces, impeding the exchange of water and gases and consequently minimizing Barhi date weight loss. It is of particular interest that the weight loss rate of the Barhi demonstrated a subtly ascending progression with the incremental addition of HCD up to 25 % in the GCP-HCD2 composite materials, a pattern approximating that of the control sample. In contrast, the polyethylene sample exhibited negligible weight decrement due to heightened malleability and water saturation, a trend that aligned with the films' barrier efficacy and architectural features [27,44].

Softening in Barhi primarily arises from the loss of hydration and the enzymatic degradation of pectin and other cell wall components through extracellular pectolytic activity. Moreover, the fruit's firmness is correlated with intercellular contact, cell wall robustness, and cellular hydrostatic pressure [45,46]. A downward trajectory in fruit firmness was noted among all samples Fig. 7b, with the subsequent storage period revealing the following firmness hierarchy for the Barhi, in descending order of magnitude: GCP-HCD1 film group, GCP film group, GCP-HCD2 group, control group, PolyEth group. The preceding assessment of barrier properties reveals that the GCP and GCP-HCD1 composite films possessed a tendency to inhibit water vapor infiltration, thus curtailing respiration and other metabolic reactions [32]. The potential for augmented enzymatic activity (specifically pectin hydrolysis), moisture retention, and ethylene-mediated softening within the polyethylene environment induces decreased textural firmness, thus scientifically explaining the observed mass retention and excessive softening of Barhi dates stored in PolyEth bags.

The concentration of total soluble solids (TSS) provides a measure of Barhi date maturation and organoleptic quality [44]. The soluble solids concentration (SSC) at the onset, control day, and during 30 days of cold storage at 4 °C is depicted in Fig. 7c, with statistical analysis demonstrating significant differences (p ≤ 0.05) in SSC concentration among the treatments; a progressive elevation in SSC was noted in all fruits throughout the storage duration, concurrent with maturation and the loss of hydration and the intrinsic metabolic process whereby carbohydrates are converted into sugars and other soluble compounds. The PolyEth group exhibited the highest SSC (31.7–49.23 %), followed by the control (31.7–47.32 %) and GCP-HCD2 (31.7–46.79 %) groups; the lowest SSC (31.7–41.21 %), observed in the GCP-HCD1 group, is likely attributable to its HCD concentration, which, due to the film relatively low WVP and antioxidant properties, likely impedes Barhi transpiration and respiration [7]; additionally, the GCP group exhibited significantly lower (p ≤ 0.05) SSC after 15 and 30 days [27,47,48]. Chitosan-coated mangoes similarly showed lower soluble solids than controls. In papaya and Barhi dates, chitosan also effectively controlled soluble solids fluctuations during five weeks' storage, consistent with its effects on reducing respiration, delaying ripening, and slowing TSS increase [47]. Liu, Cui [49] attributed this to delayed soluble solids dissolution by composite film (CDs), forming a dense surface barrier that inhibited microbial growth and respiratory metabolism in stored shiitake mushrooms, thus retarding TSS decline.

Fig. 7d demonstrates a decline in Barhi date titratable acidity with increasing storage time across all treatments; however, the GCP-HCD1 film effectively mitigated changes in acidity during storage, likely due to the potential of enzymatic activity and fruit ripening to increase Barhi pH during storage and distribution, leading to reduced acid content. The application of GCP films incorporating HCDs is anticipated to exert a positive influence on fruit attributes, thereby contributing to the extension of shelf life and the preservation of fruit quality throughout the postharvest supply chain [6,18].

This investigation details the design and characterization of novel composite films assembled from gum arabic (G), chitosan (C), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) incorporating Hibiscus flower-derived carbon dots (HCDs). The hydrothermally synthesized HCDs exhibited a spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution (predominantly 5–7 nm) and demonstrated pronounced UV absorption at 247 nm and 279 nm, along with photoluminescence at 392 nm upon 316 nm excitation. Spectrophotometric analysis revealed a concentration-dependent influence of HCDs on film color, with increasing HCD content correlating with decreased lightness and whiteness index, and increased yellowness and total color difference. Mechanistically, HCD incorporation enhanced film tensile strength and elongation (optimally at 15 % HCD loading), while higher concentrations (25 %) led to increased film thickness, suggesting a potential plasticizing effect. Furthermore, HCD incorporation diminished film solubility and moisture content, with the highest concentration exhibiting the lowest values. Notably, HCD incorporation markedly augmented the films' antioxidant capacity, as evidenced by potent DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging exceeding 85 % for the 25 % HCD film. However, this higher HCD loading also resulted in a significant increase in water vapor permeability, suggesting a potential trade-off between antioxidant and barrier properties. Postharvest storage trials with Barhi dates demonstrated the efficacy of the 15 % HCD-modified film in preserving fruit quality, significantly retarding firmness loss, minimizing weight loss, and modulating changes in soluble solids content and titratable acidity compared to control films and conventional storage methods. The data presented herein demonstrate future prospects of HCD-incorporated GCP films as advanced packaging materials for extending the shelf life of perishable produce.

Huda Ahmad Alamer: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision. Samar M.S. Shawir: Writing – original draft. Reham M. Kamel: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Abdel-Moety Salama: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Hazem Sakr: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researches Supporting Project number (PNURSP2025R894), Princess Nourah Bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.142723.
